title: 2025/12/01 - PP (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-01 - Primary Production Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries [Part 2]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries
description: Monday 1 December 2025 | Primary Production Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries Hearing of evidence: Todd McClay, Minister of Agriculture and Minister of Forestry 2024/25 Annual review of Landcorp Farming Limited (Pāmu Farms) Hearing of evidence: Landcorp Farming Limited (Pāmu) John Rae, Chair Mark Leslie, CEO Will Burrett, COO
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry for Primary Industries. Todd McClay, Minister of Agriculture and Minister of Forestry. 2024/25 Annual review of Landcorp Farming Limited (Pāmu Farms)

chunks.text: Oh, the last person in's gonna miss here. Cheers. These chairs. Yeah. Chair. There, there. Chair here. Someone wants there. Well, I always had the primary Industries committee with standing room only. That's Right. That's right. He's a chair there. Could you move?

Still coming. Should have thrown so there on the floor. That'll be fine. I think it's, is he very good? Is he here later in the week? Uh, I think so. Well, welcome to the primary production select committee. Um, we are talking around forestry.

Um, so Minister, I'll leave it in your hands. We've got you for 45 minutes. Yeah. Just clarification, Mr. Chair. Forestry Only. No, no. We go here for Everything. Put everything. Sorry. AG and Forestry. Yeah. Sorry. Um, so fire away minister. Thank you so much. Well, colleagues, uh, good afternoon, and thanks for having me back.

I, I have a few opening remarks, but I think, uh, what was most useful for both sides and maybe as the public watching is the conversation we have and, and so on. So, happy to get into that. First, can I start by thanking our, uh, the men and women of the primary sector of New Zealand, our farmers and our foresters and the fishing, uh, people and others for their hard work and their significant contribution to not only the New Zealand economy, but to feeding millions of people all around the world?

I have the, you know, the privilege of spending a bit of time overseas, both as trade and agriculture minister together. And I've gotta say that actually as the Minister of Agriculture, uh, overseas, my job is very easy. Uh, New Zealand farmers and across the primary sector are, uh, held in high regard. Uh, uh, they, uh, innovate. They work extremely effectively.

They produce the highest quality, safest food and fiber you could find almost anywhere in the world. And there are many countries who want to emulate them and follow them. As a minister of trade, my job is very difficult because the world is a place where there is still a lot of protectionism and many countries, governments are asked to protect against the, you know, productive nature of New Zealand farmers.

But that's actually a good problem to have. Uh, and, um, you know, I just want to recognize that as the economy start to recover as a men and woman of provincial New Zealand, who as always are helping dig us out of what was the challenge for all New Zealand. And so every New Zealand is better off because of the New Zealand primary, uh, sector.

Um, they are continuing to improve. You'll see in so of which we have an extra round of announcements soon, the forecast of performance of the primary sector coming up. Uh, we've hit a fixed $60 billion worth of, uh, production exports, uh, for the first time. And I would expect that we'll continue to see that, uh, grow.

That is a story both of quality, uh, uh, but it's, you know, it is the amount that we produce, but is the value that New Zealanders get from that as they sell overseas? You would've seen the government across the board has, um, you know, set the ambitious target of doubling the value of exports over 10 years. I want to recognize that, uh, the opposition, the Labor Party has joined us in that, uh, last year and saying if Admiral would also work towards that.

I think that's important because, uh, you know, it's not just about how much you produce. We have the ability to sustainably produce more whilst meeting our international and domestic, uh, climate and environment obligations. But it's about the value that we receive for that, which comes directly to, um, to innovation. Uh, and, um, you know, 60 bi, $60.4 billion, 30% increase over the previous year of exports from the primary sector.

Not only puts money in the pockets of every New Zealanders directly and indirectly, but it continues to create the, uh, create jobs. But secondly, um, um, uh, Mr. Chair, since last I was before you, uh, we have worked, uh, hard with stakeholders across the board and parties in Parliament to get the balance between, um, uh, the regulations that are needed and must be there, uh, and not, uh, just adding cost to productive businesses in New Zealand.

I don't want to recognize significant leadership's been shown by, uh, farming organizations in working with the government, in effect many parties across our parliament to help us understand and get the balance right. You know, if you take our announcement, uh, uh, not so long ago around, um, farmer emissions, it's clear farmers have an obligation to help us meet.

Uh, our net zero target of obligation are by 2050, but we shouldn't be doing things that merely close down farms and make New Zealanders produce less. And the reason for that is less carbon efficient food producers around the world will produce that food. It's called leakage. Our economy will worse off, the food will still be produced, and climate is worse off.

So we have taken a very focused, uh, scientific, uh, uh, approach to this. And I want to, uh, thank the leaders in the farming community for actually joining us and working away at us all being reasonable. They have come out and said it is a challenge to do what the government has asked, but it's based on science and we will work to do Albert.

And I think that's all we could ask on it. The most important point here is across the board. People are now saying that we must meet our obligation and we should work towards that. Final bit before I stop, um, is that we, well, we continue to invest in innovation. Started when we're in government last time, continued on the previous government. We keep doing that. The way that we meet our obligations is through science and innovation.

It is through backing New Zealanders across the board to find solutions to problems. You know, it's a very clear strategy, strategy the government has. We want to produce this more with using the same amount or less. That's what production being productive is. Uh, and, uh, you know, we must continue to do that. But the government has a role, uh, through, uh, partnering, investing with not doing for, and forestry.

Uh, we have seen for the first time forestry sector export a billion dollars worth of processed, uh, uh, forestry products. That is a significant achievement. And I think we need to continue to find, find ways to incentivize 'em, which isn't cash in itself, but incentivize 'em to innovate and find products that we can sell overseas.

You know, the government doesn't own, um, trees or forests, so it is for the forest owners to decide what they want to do with that wood. Many choose to send logs overseas, in part because of the type of, know, the, the, the way the trees have been, uh, have been treated as they have been planted and they've grown. But if we can find more ways through innovation to add value and create jobs in New Zealand, open up markets around the world, so those products can be sold, uh, you know, with the value added that's good

for the economy across the board. But it's particularly good for our regions. My approach, uh, over the last year and forestry has been the same in other parts of the primary sector. We want better rules and smarter rules. Not what, just ones that hold back. Uh, and that, uh, create costs without a clear, uh, benefit of outcome.

The forestry sector has worked with us very, very closely, and I'm extremely grateful, uh, to them. They have provided advice, uh, and examples of their experience that helps us get the rules right. We have to get the rules right. Governments has obligations area, but they already have stepped up to work with us. You'll have know, uh, when, uh, we came to government, we inherited a court case.

The government was being sued over the, uh, the emissions, uh, uh, trading scheme, forestry register. We were able to work through that and get the cost down and get in a much better space by setting up a industry group to work with us that for case was not settled, it was dropped. And, and they, they decided not to go ahead, uh, with it. And that's gotta be good for us.

'cause all of our energy on both sides now has been put into better rules and regulations and how we can work together. Forestry has a very important role to play in New Zealand. It not only helps us meet our, our short to medium term, uh, climate obligation, but it creates a lot of jobs in regions of New Zealand. In some cases, there are fewer other jobs, but we need to be as productive and innovative and forestry as we are in every other part of the primary sector.

I recently, uh, was involved in a trade mission to India from the forestry sector, both processed, uh, um, uh, timber and forestry products, as well as those that grow trees. And the opportunity that that group saw there to work with the government to add value to what we do in New Zealand and open up opportunities in new markets was almost unprecedented.

It shows me that we shouldn't treat forestry any different than any other part of the primary sector. Had the opportunity to produce income for New Zealanders and create jobs in New Zealand. And if we can help them, uh, with better, smarter rules to achieve what they need to, and at the same time, uh, um, we, um, you know, open up new markets, then the delivery from the wider primary sector will continue to be something New Zealand should be proud of.

And with that in mind, uh, happy to take, uh, comments and questions. Thanks minister. We're going straight to Steve Abel for first's. Question. Thank you, Mr. Chair. Um, minister, good to have you here. This is sort of a crossover issue question, uh, that relates both to agriculture and trade. Um, live of animal exports is something that your government has, uh, touted It would be doing work on and, and having reports complete on, uh, this past year.

Um, it both impacts agricultural production here and also trade. What's going on with that? And have you kicked it to touch? No. So, uh, minister Hogan's responsible, it's one of the delegations, and you have a next, I let him out to that more substantially, but there's still a commitment on the part of the government. We campaigned on it. We, we did say we want to get it right.

Uh, there are opportunities for parts of the agricultural, you know, production to be able to meet demand overseas. Uh, and there is interest in, you know, live animals. But we were very clear the only way animals would be able to leave under regulatory regime we set up in New Zealand is it met the very high standards that, uh, we have set in New Zealand.

And New Zealanders would expect to run under animal husbandry and animal welfare, the way animals would be treated on any shift that left the purpose-built ships, and also in the country they go to. So, Mr. Hogar has been working at that, uh, and he'll be in a better best place to tell you, uh, you know, what the next steps are, but it's not something that stepped back from, we were very clear though, we said that we would get that balance right.

And, uh, no animals should leave New Zealand until we have arrived at that point of that very high level of assurance. And the work that he's been doing with the sector and with officials is commendable. But you'll be able to, uh, talk to in greater Detail, supple. Yep. Yep. So you said you'd been on a trade mission recently, so I understand as, as recently as, uh, August you visited Indonesia, and I presume that would've been with your foreign affairs trade add on, but perhaps also with your minister. It Was, it was both. And it wasn't

raised in a single meeting I was at. Yeah. So, uh, there's an, sorry. The live Animal exports wasn't raised in a single meeting I was at, uh, when I was in Indonesian. Okay. So that's interesting because in October, Indonesian media recorded, ordered the, um, cooperation between the two countries, includes plans for the import of superior livestock such as dairy cows.

And Hamish Ma, a representative for the New Zealand Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade, expressed his country's support and readiness to supply live cattle to Indonesia. Is that Incorrect? That, so that gentleman doesn't work for Infa, but he, uh, is the, um, site, which is the, um, special Special Agricultural trading. Yeah. And he has opportunity to go and look, talk to different markets.

Well, look, I I'm not gonna comment on what the media said there anymore than I probably would comment on the media in New Zealand, but it wasn't raised in meetings I was at. There were conversations about how we can help, um, uh, Indonesia with food production. And one of the things that's very interesting, if you look at some of the vegetables they may produce, uh, from, you know, fruit and other things to, uh, animals and, and meat or dairy, there are many things New Zealand does around the world around innovation.

So New Zealand farmers, you know, produce some of the highest quality produce around the world because of systems we have in place, and animal feed and so on. Uh, Indonesia does import, um, live animals from Australia. They don't from New Zealand. Uh, should, uh, uh, you know, minister hoard, uh, um, you know, uh, uh, think that a request for legislation was in place, a request from Indonesia, they, as every other country would from the world, would have to meet the very, very high standards.

But one of the ways, uh, today we could help Indonesia, not only with their beef production, dairy production, is genetics. And that doesn't have to mean live animal. My final one just share would be, um, does exporting that superior livestock, uh, in the form of genetics as live animals, um, such as dairy cows in the instance of the Indonesian report, what threat does that pose to our point of difference?

And is that the best use of our genetic? So before we go on, I'm not gonna accept the premise of the question that says, suppose live animals are leaving. It's illegal in New Zealand. I've been pretty clear there are many ways we can help Indonesia and improve their production to feed people more. We can sell them more food, and we can help them with genetics, which doesn't have to mean, doesn't mean live animals.

If you look at how we have improved, um, uh, livestock in New Zealand, some of it has been through selective genetic breeding within New Zealand. Some of it has been the importation, uh, often of, uh, not animals, but of seamen and embryos and other things. So there are many ways to do this. Your question, though, see, is should we put ourselves up in Fortress New Zealand and pretend that we can just sell to the world?

Or do we have to continue to make the case that we help others for better access to markets and they should be able to feed themselves? New Zealand produces enough food to feed 40 million people around the world. I, I think it's better to say 400 million people get 10% of their diet from New Zealand. It doesn't matter how much we produce. We couldn't feed the whole world.

So we had to continue to look at the value of what we do and how we can go up in the value chain. Our exporters through the producers are very, very good at that. But there are many ways we create jobs in New Zealand, helping others be more productive. It's not something we should be afraid of. Mr. You gotta, you've gotta sub there. Mark. Uh, yes. It's actually back on, on, on minister's question, uh, and statement, if that's all right?

Yep. Sure. Thank you, Mr. Chair. And, and minister, good to see you on online. Apologies for my absence. I'm, uh, in isolation as it were. Um, we talk about doubling of revenue, export earnings, and to your point, when we, we congratulate New Zealanders as being innovative, and we certainly are, do you see that being the area which would be the largest to, to, to fill in that hole as it were, you know, doubling revenue, export earnings is a, is a hell of a task?

Or is it a case that we just produce more? Or, uh, third, third sort of question statement? Is it a combination of both? Yeah. So, so there are two ways to earn more. One is to work twice as hard. The other was to work smarter. So our, our proposal before the election, or a commitment, and it has come through governance, has doubled the value of exports.

Uh, it was doubled the value of exports, right? The amount, not the amount we export, but, uh, the value that we get for it now. I think it's a bit of a combination of both. There are, there are opportunities for us to, um, add value to the production. We have, you know, Fonterra is a pretty good example of that. When you go to the China market, uh, they have taken a, a, um, a, uh, a cream product that is for, um, use and, um, food production, uh, and has a greater value, uh, uh, and produces more cream, uh, when it is used for whipping

or other things, uh, um, you know, cakes and sandwiches, whatever, as they put it in, uh, for the amount of dairy product that goes in it. That's innovation. And that actually has been exceptionally, uh, beneficial to the Chinese market to Fonterra, which flows straight back to, um, you know, producers in New Zealand.

So I think the answer is, it can be both. Is this a play that says it's just double the amount of dairy or meat that we produce? It certainly isn't. And you know, there are many examples. Now, if we take meat where we don't compete on price and markets as we used to, it is on providence. It's an innovation. It's the quality and reputation of that meat.

It is on many, many things. And I think it's a combination of those two I made. It's very clear though, that there are environmental obligations that we have in New Zealand. The government sets, uh, doesn't allow us to double how much we sell by doubling the amount that we produce. So innovation, uh, finding ways to do more with the same amount or less is the biggest part of the, you know, the, the story of us being able to sell more value overseas.

And that's part of the reason why the government previous and current, uh, works to invest in partnership to drive greater innovation. Not only the farm. Great. But through food processing and export Minister, very quickly, are you confident invariably governments do change and you've, you've worked across the aisle to make sure that the kind of language that a future government that potentially is not one that we share, uh, is of the same volition you, you're confident in that, correct? Well, Well look, um, I mean, I think in trade

and agriculture, we should find ways to work across the aisle. There will be differences in which is political because it's parliament, political people, but you know, New Zealand farmers need all of our backing. Doesn't mean they're allowed to do what they want. No government ever allowed that. But we need to, uh, make sure that there is a greater amount of, certain, greater amount of certainty, both around primary, uh, the primary sector and also in our export markets.

But in case that doesn't work out, I'm working very hard. So we get another three years. Brilliant. Right. Uh, Joe, thank you. Thank you. Thank you, um, for coming to the select committee minister. I just wanted to, I've got lots of questions, but I'll, I'll just, um, ask them as the chair allows it. Um, It's very tough.

Um, on, in the, um, NPI annual report, um, assessment of operations overview for 24 25, it talks about, um, only 82% of the agricultural programs supporting sustainability were achieved. And I'm just wondering if you're able to, um, if not, you can come back to us maybe later on, but what programs weren't delivered and what were the barriers to those?

Yeah, yeah. Being delivered completely. So, So in some cases not fully achieved working on work under progress and so on. I mean, sustainability is very, very important. Uh, um, but it's not the only thing we need to do. We, we sometimes, we generally end up focusing on sustainably more than what we're trying to do, which is be as productive as we can be. But that doesn't mean we should be producing, uh, you know, um, in a way that is not sustainable.

Um, but I'll, I'll pass to, to Ray perhaps to go into that a little bit more about some of the detail. But I know you have 'em here later in the week, so to really get into that he can help. Yeah. Which might be a better, might be a better idea. I mean, there's, you'll know, there's, um, I think there's, NPI i's got about 120 different outputs on these things. So I, I can't tell you exactly off Yeah, those, which bits of those we missed.

Um, generally speaking, um, for each of those appropriation lines, there's an attempt to get some measurements on them. Um, some, some of 'em are a bit harder to achieve than others. Some of 'em are about whether you kind of get enough programs across the line, others are more, are much more substantive in terms of the volumes that we might be processing in particular areas of the business.

Um, so ha happy to go through those in a bit of detail if, if, if you wanted. But I, I suspect, um, I suspect in those goals, they'll be largely about, um, investments and programs that we are making. Okay. So Joe, no. Can I ask new one since then, Cathy? Okay. Okay, cool. Thank you.

Um, now I know this po this isn't specifically necessarily just your delegation I'm about to ask you about, but as agriculture minister, I do think you have a, a responsibility probably over this, and it's around the issue with our wilding pines, uh, taking over really, um, productive or potential to continue to take over productive farmland and the conversations around, uh, returning grazing to some dock land as well.

I'm interested to know Yeah. Where you are thinking it is, is at with that, because I think given that we've had a, a reduction in the, um, the funding for attacking wilding Pines, How have we had a reduction These Things? We put some more, we put more money into the budget, an extra $2 million. Well, my understanding is that it's been when I get no, when I get up and talk to people on the ground, they say that there is not as much funding. So, So, so before we go on, I can help you because otherwise we end up, But let me finish my question first.

I'm just interested to, makes extra 2 million what you're doing in that space. That's An increase, not a decrease, But those farmers that are being affected are saying that there's less so, so as Politicians, Whoever's right, it doesn't matter. But I'm just wanted to know, I know it's not specifically, you've got other ministers that have those delegations, but I'm really interested to hear your views on, on That. So, so it's not a delegation, it's administered by securities.

It's not a allegation. He's specifically responsible that a delegation would be if I was, and I gave him something like, uh, animal welfare. So it's, he's responsible and you'll have them beforehand shortly, but I just want to correct that. You may be having people saying there is a cut, there is not, there's extra money putting extra $2 million into, uh, wild pines, uh, budget this year, which, which helps.

So, so, but I am taking an interest for two reasons. Yeah. More broadly, um, uh, in the case of agriculture, but I'm forestry minister, so anything that happened. So interestingly, if you think about wilding Pine, there's been a lot in the media around this. If you look at, um, uh, some of the most recent media around that, that was a purposeful government decision many, many years ago to, you know, 20 years ago Yep.

To go from airplanes and drop trees everywhere in an area to stop erosions, to grow forests. Yep. So that's not wilding pines as a result of that. There can be wilding pines, but it's not that vast parts of the countryside. In the case of what you saw on TV as a result of wilding pines, it was a conscious decision that property wasn't the best one of those years ago. Uh, uh, second, secondly, um, there are two opportunities that we need to see that the cost.

And so what I've said to the agency, so I wouldn't argue that the extra money we put in is enough to meet the problem. I'm not saying it is. I'm just saying that we saw the issue coming and gave more so that actually we could start to look to do more and gear up. I have asked 'em to go and look at what the money is being spent on before the case is made for more, because I want to make sure it's being used effectively.

It's not just on the management of idea and strategies. Effectively dealing with this, uh, minister Hoggard, when he comes before you may well talk about go working with feds, federated farmers and other foreign farming groups, as well as environmental groups about priorities to target and the best way to do it.

But also looking at doing different types of trials to, to, to get rid of areas where wilding pines are starting to, to take over. Uh, and some of those may well be successful, others not. The final bit of your question is around, um, you know, grazing and so on. I'll be very careful there because the Ministry of Conservation has responsibility, what can do, it's guided by an act and so on.

But there are likely to be some very small scale controlled trials to see the effect that grazing may have upon, um, um, upon, you know, that part of the countryside and wilding pines and so on. Don't wanna jump ahead and say what that looks like. Yeah. There are a lot of farmers who tell me it's great, just as there are farmers telling you they're better cut.

So let's take that with a grain of salt and sea. But the point of this is before we say this is the amount that might be needed, what do we take it away from to reprioritize rather than borrowing money or taxing more? Uh, we need to make sure the money that's there's being spent will get the most that it can. You will see us having put in place, uh, very, uh, strict rules around full farm to forest conversion.

Mm-hmm. Right? And it was supported across parliament. I'm very grateful for that because, uh, again, it's given, it's given, it's given a lot of certainty that in itself, uh, uh, you know, uh, could well have a positive impact, right? Uh, fewer trees being planted, but ultimately there were parts of the countryside where a lot of work's got, needs to be done very, very quickly because, you know, you get to the point where it could be outta control.

There's other areas where it's more managed over a period of time. So the thinking very quickly is going into that by the other two ministers, Steve? Yes. Up. Yeah. It's a sub. I mean, you would acknowledge that Wilding Pines is a wicked problem. It's had a, has a ravenous ability to consume land, which is both bad for production and for biodiversity and for clarity, the, the cut down to $10 million that your government made in 2024 was a cut from the Jobs for Nature funding.

You've increased it to $12 million. But, um, the wilding pine networks is, it needs to be 25 million per year over the next decade. So the current funding is less than half of what's needed. Um, Southland, uh, regional council environment, Southland said that without adequate funding, wild and pine control work will go backwards with cleared land prime for reinfestation and also further expansion across the region.

So are are you really taking this problem seriously? Most, yeah. Taking serious, but a couple of things. A couple of things. First, jobs and nature wasn't jobs and nature wasn't baseline funding, it was limited. If you wanted it to be there forever, your government could have put it in there. It wasn't. So that's the way that not My government, Well, you were in government as a green mp. Uh, I wasn't. No, well, Your party was, and you're a green MP now, unless there's some other stuff you'd like to divorce yourself from they did as well.

Right. So the point of this is, the point of this is, right, But your government chose before It wasn't baseline funding, if you want be there, you set up it is baseline funding. The $2 million is baseline funding. It's not there for four years and disappear. So I can come back sometime in the future and say it was a cut. It wasn't a cut, it was a decision on the part of the last government to have a time bound funding. So Let me clarify my question. $25 million needed annually for 10 years. So, so you, so they have come out

and said $25 million that probably needs some head checking, but before we can look at any more money, I wanna make sure it's gonna achieve something other than 20, spending $25 million. There are many ways to be able to, to get wilding pines away. You know, largely there is a view out there that is radiator pine. That's a problem that generally it is not. Right.

And so it is not from productive production forests in New Zealand. It's through many other things. Right. And that's where we're gonna be very careful because there is a big argument that's that's grown in the public, that is foresters who are responsible for this foresters have to be responsible for what they do, including on other people's land. In this case, the challenges, you know, wider.

This here is an example of something that could help in the future. And I almost am hesitant to do this. Uh, you know, there's technology around the world with CRISPR that allows you to turn something off, which is not, uh, uh, you know, an introduction of something else genetic. That means these trees in the future have to be planted, you know, have to be propagated rather than, you know, the spread of, of seeds and so on and, and pollen.

There are many technologies we don't have available to us today that we should have the ability to consider to see whether it may help with wilding pines or other pest. So, so, you know, that's part of the, the biotech bill that comes through that gives us that cut that to consider it. But what I would say is that as far the amount of money is concerned, uh, there is a debate and discussion going on with a wide group of stakeholders about how we might meet the cost both in labor and the actual cost of dealing with a growing wilding pine problem.

I agree with you. I don't think we should assume for any one moment, it is purely the taxpayer that should meet that cost when we can see the best way to deal with in different circumstances and that we are getting the most trees out for the money that is committed. Uh, I would happily make the case for some more, but until I am clear of that, just me committing what someone else says we should spend may not be the best use of money today.

Yeah. Look, we'll move on from, um, this subject 'cause it really is plenty of others. And I like to remind you that I got there, there is a bill in, in the, in the members ballot that deals with this whose got A wild piece. Yeah. A wild Next.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. Minister. Um, it's positive to hear about your, um, trade and export, um, discussions. However, some forest, particularly north of Toko Maori Bay, are still finding the cost of getting to port Yeah. Prohibitive. So have you got any examples of any farms or trusts or companies you're working with north of Tomo Bay to alleviate that?

And many people will appreciate your use of the word incentives, um, particularly when we are talking about land use. Um, what incentives have you got in place, um, and have you got any examples of any of those being delivered? Yeah, so, so Sophie, just to come to your first, uh, question, um, about when it costs a lot to get product to market and get it overseas and so on, uh, we, we have to think more deeply about, uh, where investment is put and the infras infrastructure may be and how you would get it to a market.

There are examples in that part of the country though where, um, where, um, uh, saw mills and others are looking to use wood and maybe add value, process it more closely. Uh, there's a great example, uh, an air gson of a saw mill that closed, that was bought by Australians that up and running. They're actually, um, talking to government not about, uh, funding incentive, but the regulatory settings where they can do some code generation there for their own plant and to share in the grid more widely that they say that they're using it in Australia elsewhere

and can work very well in New Zealand. And they will take any fiber that they can get. I think what you're gonna see over the next sort of five to 10 years is much more interest in the value of the fiber and the trees that we've been growing than necessarily the log and the use for it. Now that doesn't help, that doesn't help, you know, the forests you're talking about right now, but it is something that's very, very interesting.

So I think in a couple of areas through the, um, the, the, um, primary sector growth fund, uh, which, uh, um, you know, was we have changed and refocus. Government has some money. Uh, we are not the lion's share of it and that greater use and other types of use of wood is something we're interested in. The second thing is, if you think about wood processing, right? I think it changes in the many years here. So largely, uh, you know, it's the log that's expensive to get the port and get overseas. If value is added to it, even just put it through a sawmill and treat it, the value goes up. or have a better road to get to that port. It's how can we add value right here? So what we get is a lot more, and it's as simple as that. I don't wanna get it wrong, but, but you know, um, there are people now who are saying, we can plant trees and grow them for six years and make more money if we grow them for 20.
